one_line_takeaway: DarkCite weaponizes LLMs' trust in authoritative-looking (but fabricated) citations to jailbreak aligned models in a black-box, low-query setting, topping SOTA average ASR (66%) -- though it stalls against well-aligned Claude-3, and a simple citation-harm-verification system prompt restores most of the defense (DPR 11%->74%).
key_finding: On AdvBench, DarkCite achieves the highest average ASR (66%) among 8 attacks, beating the prior SOTA PAP (61%) by ~8 points and doing so with only 3 victim queries versus PAP's 3x40 and GCG/AutoDAN's 3x100. Per-model DarkCite ASR: Llama2-7b 76%, GPT-3.5-turbo 97%, GPT-4 83%, Claude-3 7%. The proposed harm-verification mitigation raises average DPR from 11% (default template) to 74%. Logit-distribution analysis (KL divergence, t-SNE) supports the claim that citation-based jailbreaks behave like an implicit RAG attack.
core_contribution: The paper introduces DarkCite, a black-box jailbreak attack that exploits what the authors name LLMs' 'authority bias' -- an inherent tendency to trust authoritative-looking citations (fake academic papers, GitHub repos, Wikipedia entries). DarkCite is a three-stage pipeline: (1) a risk-citation matching classifier that maps a harmful instruction's risk type to the citation source most likely to succeed against it; (2) an LLM-driven generator that fabricates a structured, credible-looking citation whose content is aligned with the harmful intent but stripped of trigger keywords; (3) a harmfulness assessment using Llama-Guard-3. The paper's own additional contributions are: an empirical characterization of authority bias (Finding 1: academic/GitHub citations jailbreak more effectively than blogs/news; Finding 2: risk-specific source susceptibility, e.g. malware trusts GitHub, attributed to uneven pre-training data distribution), a hypothesis that DarkCite functions as an 'implicit RAG attack' (supported by KL-divergence and t-SNE logit similarity between cited and with-RAG responses), and a system-prompt-based defense combining authenticity verification and citation-harm verification.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with no access to the victim model's architecture, parameters, gradients, or output logits -- only input/output query access. The attacker embeds fabricated authoritative citations into an otherwise-refused harmful instruction to bypass safety alignment and content filters, using a separate attacker-controlled LLM (Baichuan-13B offline or GPT-4o online) to generate the fake citations. Framed as a red-team / responsible-disclosure effort; defenses (system-prompt verification) are also proposed. Attack is claimed to be efficient (3 trials per prompt, few victim queries, low token cost) versus iterative/gradient methods.
limitations: Claude-3-haiku largely resists DarkCite (only 7% ASR), so the attack is far weaker against strongly aligned models, and PAP actually edges DarkCite on GPT-4 (88% vs 83%). The causal Finding 2 (pre-training distribution causes source-specific susceptibility) is inferential: distributions were estimated by keyword-matching on proxy open datasets, not the victims' actual training corpora, so it is correlational rather than demonstrated. The 'implicit RAG attack' claim is explicitly a hypothesis backed only by logit-similarity evidence, not a mechanistic proof. The mitigation is uneven -- on weakly aligned Vicuna, harm-verification DPR reaches only 13% (from 0%). HEx-PHI ablation uses only 10 sub-sampled questions per category. The paper is also visibly a rough draft (numerous grammatical errors and inconsistent figure references), which lowers confidence in some reported details.

datasets_used: AdvBench (520 harmful behaviors) -- primary evaluation and defense tests
HEx-PHI (11 risk categories, 10 examples each; 10 sampled per category following ArtPrompt) -- ablation across citation types and risk scenarios
quantitative_results: DarkCite average ASR on AdvBench: 66% (highest of 8 methods)
DarkCite per-model ASR: Llama2-7b 76%, GPT-3.5-turbo 97%, GPT-4 83%, Claude-3 7%
SOTA baseline PAP average ASR: 61% (Llama-2 68%, GPT-3.5 86%, GPT-4 88%, Claude-3 0%)
DarkCite uses only 3 victim-model trials vs GCG/AutoDAN 3x100 and PAP 3x40

Defense ASR (Table 2) baseline no-defense: Llama-2 76%, Llama-3 45%, GPT-3.5 97%, GPT-4 83%; RA-LLM alone drops Llama-2 to 29%, Llama-3 to 7%; all three defenses combined drop Llama-2 to 18%, Llama-3 to 5%
Perplexity filter barely reduces ASR (Llama-2 76%->76%, Llama-3 45%->44%); OpenAI Moderation removes only ~26% of harmful content

Mitigation DPR (Table 3): default 11% avg -> authenticity-verification 52% avg -> harm-verification 74% avg; harm-verification per model: Llama-2 86%, Vicuna 13%, GPT-3.5 98%, GPT-4 99%; Vicuna improves 0%->13%
baselines_compared: PAP (persuasive adversarial prompts) -- 61% avg ASR (prior SOTA)
Defenses evaluated: OpenAI Moderation, RA-LLM, Perplexity Filter
